text.title: Cialdini's Principles, Tactical Moves, and Handling Hard Situations
text.summary: Cialdini's six principles of persuasion with business applications, five negotiation tactics (anchoring, silence, flinch, bracketing, nibble), and scripts for handling hardball situations.

rule.statement: Apply at most two Cialdini principles per message — stacking all six reads as a sales script and triggers skepticism.
rule.rationale: Each principle works because it operates below conscious evaluation. Stacking them makes the pattern obvious, which activates resistance rather than compliance.

list.item: Reciprocity: give value first — offer a free audit, report, or consultation before asking for anything
Commitment: get small yes agreements early — "Do you agree quality is important?" → "Does our approach improve quality?" → "Shall we proceed?"
Social Proof: "Companies like [similar company] saw 40% improvement"
Authority: cite credentials, data, and research — not just assertions
Liking: find genuine common ground before disagreeing
Scarcity: "Only 2 project slots left this quarter" — only use if true

rule.statement: Anchor first with a specific number, not a range — ranges anchor on the favorable end for the counterparty.
rule.rationale: First numbers set the psychological reference point for the entire negotiation. Ranges like "$80K-$100K" anchor at $80K in the buyer's mind.

rule.statement: After making an offer, stop talking — silence makes the counterparty fill the void, often with a concession.
rule.rationale: Discomfort with silence causes most negotiators to soften their position before they need to. Holding silence is leverage that costs nothing.

example.note: Bracketing to reach a target number.
example.code: Target: $90K Their offer: $80K Counter: $100K Expected midpoint: $90K ← your target

rule.statement: Use the nibble (asking for small extras after the main agreement) only after the deal is signed, not before.
rule.rationale: Pre-agreement nibbles signal that you are still bargaining on the main deal, which reopens closed points. Post-agreement nibbles are small enough that the counterparty's sunk cost prevents reopening.

rule.statement: Respond to "Take it or leave it" by exploring the constraint behind the position, not by accepting or walking out.
rule.rationale: "Take it or leave it" is almost always a posture, not a real constraint. Exploring what created the constraint often surfaces a creative option neither party had considered.

example.code: "I understand this is your position. Help me understand the constraints that led to it. Maybe we can find another way to meet your needs while also addressing mine."
"Too expensive" → "Compared to what?" "Over budget" → "What is your budget?" "Cheaper elsewhere" → "What else is different?"

antipattern.reason: First offers are anchors, not final positions. Accepting immediately signals that the offer was too generous, which the counterparty will remember in future negotiations.
Email removes tone and timing signals, which are 60-80% of negotiation information. Use email for documenting agreements, not for reaching them.
